1:
"And I saw when the Lamb opened one of the seals, and I heard, as it were the noise of thunder, one of the four beasts saying, Come and see." The witnesses note the Lamb opening the first seal and the summons to behold. Victorinus notes that, the first seal being opened, he saw a white horse and a crowned horseman; the Lamb who alone could open the seals now begins to unfold the sealed book. The witnesses note John seeing the Lamb open one of the seals, and hearing, as the noise of thunder, one of the four living creatures say, "Come and see"; the Lamb beginning to unloose the sealed book of God's purposes — that John, having seen the Lamb take the book (ch.5), now beholds him "open one of the seals" — the Lamb alone worthy and able to unfold the hidden counsels of God concerning the church and the world — and at the opening, "one of the four beasts" (living creatures) cries "as the noise of thunder, Come and see" (a loud, commanding summons to behold the vision that follows); the witnesses note the solemn beginning of the unsealing — the future is opened only by the Lamb, and the heavenly creatures call the seer to attend to each great scene as it is disclosed; and I heard, as it were the noise of thunder, one of the four beasts saying, Come and see. The witnesses note when the Lamb opened one of the seals. The witnesses note one of the four beasts saying, Come and see.

The witnesses note the Lamb opening the first seal — alone worthy to unfold God's hidden counsels — and the living creature's thunderous summons "Come and see" to behold the vision.

So the witnesses make the verse the Lamb beginning to unseal God's book. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Lamb alone unfolds the future, and the heavenly creatures summon the seer to behold each scene. The witnesses note the opened a seal: "when the Lamb opened one of the seals" — Victorinus notes the Lamb begins to unfold the book; the opened a seal; the the Lamb; the unfold; the the book; the begins. The expositors observe the alone worthy: the Lamb alone worthy to open it; the alone worthy; the the Lamb; the able; the the counsels; the unfold. The witnesses press the noise of thunder: "as it were the noise of thunder" — a loud, commanding sound; the noise of thunder; the loud; the commanding; the the cry; the the summons. The expositors draw the lesson on the four beasts: "one of the four beasts" — a living creature; the four beasts; the living creature; the the heavenly; the cries; the the throne. The witnesses observe the come and see: "Come and see" — a summons to behold the vision; the come and see; the the summons; the behold; the the vision; the attend. The expositors note the the future opened: the future is opened only by the Lamb; the the future opened; the only by the Lamb; the the counsels; the disclosed; the unfold. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when the Lamb opened one of the seals, the one of the four beasts saying Come and see, and the unsealing begun. The verse thus records the Lamb beginning to unseal God's book — teaching that the Lamb alone unfolds the future, and the creatures summon the seer to behold.
2:
"And I saw, and behold a white horse: and he that sat on him had a bow; and a crown was given unto him: and he went forth conquering, and to conquer." The witnesses note the first seal — the white horse and its conquering rider. Victorinus notes the white horse and crowned horseman with a bow signify the word of God, going forth in the days of the apostles to conquer; Gill takes the white horse to represent the ministration of the gospel in the apostles' times, swift, majestic, and victorious. The witnesses note John seeing a white horse, its rider with a bow and a crown, going forth conquering and to conquer; the triumphant going-forth of the conquering rider on the white horse — that John sees the first seal disclose a vision of conquest: "a white horse" (white, the colour of purity and victory) "and he that sat on him had a bow; and a crown was given unto him: and he went forth conquering, and to conquer"; the witnesses note the interpretive variety the commentators allow — some, with Victorinus and Gill, take the rider to be Christ in the victorious going-forth of his gospel in the apostolic age, conquering hearts by the word; others have read the rider as Christ himself or his cause triumphing through the ages; all agree the scene is one of victory — a crowned conqueror riding forth in unstoppable triumph; he went forth conquering, and to conquer. The witnesses note behold a white horse: and he that sat on him had a bow; and a crown was given unto him. The witnesses note he went forth conquering, and to conquer.

The witnesses note the first seal — a white horse, its crowned rider with a bow, going "conquering, and to conquer"; read by Victorinus and Gill as the victorious going-forth of the gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse the conquering rider on the white horse. The lesson the expositors draw is that the first seal shows a scene of victory, the crowned rider going forth in triumph, read as the gospel's conquest. The witnesses note the white horse: "behold a white horse" — Gill notes white, purity and victory; the white horse; the white; the purity; the victory; the the gospel. The expositors observe the the bow and crown: "had a bow; and a crown was given unto him" — armed and crowned for conquest; the the bow; the the crown; the armed; the crowned; the conquest. The witnesses press the conquering: "he went forth conquering, and to conquer" — riding forth in unstoppable triumph; the conquering; the to conquer; the triumph; the unstoppable; the victory. The expositors present the readings: the witnesses note Victorinus and Gill read the rider as Christ in the victorious going-forth of the gospel in the apostolic age, conquering hearts by the word; others as Christ or his cause triumphing through the ages. The witnesses observe the the gospel conquers: the gospel goes forth conquering hearts; the the gospel conquers; the hearts; the the word; the the apostolic age; the victory. The expositors note the a scene of victory: all agree the scene is one of victory; the a scene of victory; the all agree; the triumph; the the conqueror; the rides forth. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the behold a white horse and he that sat on him had a bow and a crown was given unto him, the he went forth conquering and to conquer, and the conquering rider. The verse thus records the conquering rider on the white horse — teaching that the first seal shows a scene of victory, read as the gospel's conquest.
3:
"And when he had opened the second seal, I heard the second beast say, Come and see." The witnesses note the opening of the second seal and the second creature's summons. Matthew Henry notes the next three seals give a sad prospect of great and desolating judgments with which God punishes those who refuse or abuse the everlasting gospel. The witnesses note John hearing the second living creature say "Come and see" as the Lamb opened the second seal; the second creature summoning the seer to the next vision of judgment — that John, as the Lamb "had opened the second seal," hears "the second beast say, Come and see" — a fresh summons, from a different one of the four living creatures, to behold the next scene; the witnesses note the orderly unfolding — each seal is opened by the Lamb in turn, and at each a heavenly creature calls the seer to attend; and Matthew Henry notes the turn that now begins — after the bright vision of the conquering gospel (the first seal), the next three seals open a darker prospect of judgments upon those who reject or abuse the gospel: war, famine, and death; so that the second creature's summons introduces the first of these woes; and when he had opened the second seal, I heard the second beast say, Come and see. The witnesses note when he had opened the second seal. The witnesses note I heard the second beast say, Come and see.

The witnesses note the second seal opened and the second creature's summons — introducing, after the bright gospel-vision, a darker prospect of judgments on those who reject the gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse the second creature summoning to the next vision. The lesson the expositors draw is that each seal is opened by the Lamb in turn, the second introducing a darker prospect of judgment. The witnesses note the second seal: "when he had opened the second seal" — the Lamb opens the second; the second seal; the the Lamb; the opens; the in turn; the unfolds. The expositors observe the second beast: "I heard the second beast say, Come and see" — a different creature summons; the second beast; the the creature; the summons; the come and see; the calls. The witnesses press the orderly: each seal opened in turn, a creature calling; the orderly; the in turn; the the creature; the calls; the unfolds. The expositors draw the lesson on Matthew Henry's note: Henry notes the next three seals open judgments on those who reject the gospel; the Henry; the the three seals; the judgments; the reject the gospel; the darker. The witnesses observe the darker prospect: after the bright vision, a darker prospect; the darker prospect; the the bright vision; the war; the famine; the death. The expositors note the the first woe: the second creature introduces the first of these woes; the the first woe; the introduces; the the woes; the judgment; the begins. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when he had opened the second seal, the I heard the second beast say Come and see, and the summons to judgment. The verse thus records the second creature summoning to the next vision — teaching that each seal is opened in turn, the second introducing a darker prospect of judgment.
4:
"And there went out another horse that was red: and power was given to him that sat thereon to take peace from the earth, and that they should kill one another: and there was given unto him a great sword." The witnesses note the second seal — the red horse of war and slaughter. Gill notes the red horse may be an emblem of the suffering state of the church, or of the contentions and slaughters that arose; its rider was given power to take peace from the earth. The witnesses note John seeing a red horse whose rider was given power to take peace from the earth and make men kill one another, with a great sword; the red horse bringing war, bloodshed, and the loss of peace — that the second seal discloses a fearful scene of strife: "another horse that was red" (red, the colour of blood and slaughter) "and power was given to him that sat thereon to take peace from the earth, and that they should kill one another: and there was given unto him a great sword"; the witnesses note the meaning the commentators draw — the going-out of this red horse signifies the coming of war, bloodshed, and mutual slaughter, peace being taken from the earth so that men turn their swords against one another; whether read of the persecutions of the church or the bloody convulsions of the empire, the scene is one of violence and the great sword of carnage; power was given to him... to take peace from the earth. The witnesses note another horse that was red... to take peace from the earth. The witnesses note that they should kill one another: and there was given unto him a great sword.

The witnesses note the second seal — the red horse of war, its rider taking peace from the earth and making men kill one another with a great sword; a scene of bloodshed and mutual slaughter.

So the witnesses make the verse the red horse of war and slaughter. The lesson the expositors draw is that the second seal brings war, bloodshed, and the loss of peace, men turning their swords on one another. The witnesses note the red horse: "another horse that was red" — Gill notes red, the colour of blood; the red horse; the red; the blood; the slaughter; the war. The expositors observe the take peace: "to take peace from the earth" — peace removed from the earth; the take peace; the removed; the the earth; the no peace; the strife. The witnesses press the kill one another: "that they should kill one another" — men turning swords on each other; the kill one another; the mutual slaughter; the swords; the strife; the carnage. The expositors draw the lesson on the great sword: "there was given unto him a great sword" — the sword of carnage; the great sword; the carnage; the slaughter; the war; the bloodshed. The witnesses observe the the readings: read of persecution of the church or convulsions of the empire; the the readings; the persecution; the the church; the the empire; the strife. The expositors note the violence: the scene is one of violence and slaughter; the violence; the slaughter; the war; the bloodshed; the the sword. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the another horse that was red to take peace from the earth, the that they should kill one another and there was given unto him a great sword, and the red horse of war. The verse thus records the red horse of war and slaughter — teaching that the second seal brings war and the loss of peace.
5:
"And when he had opened the third seal, I heard the third beast say, Come and see. And I beheld, and lo a black horse; and he that sat on him had a pair of balances in his hand." The witnesses note the third seal — the black horse and the balances. Barnes notes that the third living creature takes a part in calling attention to the new vision; the black horse and the balances signify scarcity, the careful weighing of food in time of famine. The witnesses note John seeing, at the third seal, a black horse whose rider held a pair of balances; the black horse and balances betokening scarcity and famine — that the third seal opens a vision of want: at the summons of "the third beast," John beholds "a black horse" (black, the colour of mourning and distress) "and he that sat on him had a pair of balances in his hand"; the witnesses note the symbol — the "balances" (scales) in the rider's hand betoken scarcity and dearth, for in time of famine food is doled out by careful weight, measured and rationed; the black colour fitting the gloom and grief of want; so that the third seal, following war, brings the famine that war breeds, food weighed out in scant measure; a black horse; and he that sat on him had a pair of balances in his hand. The witnesses note when he had opened the third seal... lo a black horse. The witnesses note he that sat on him had a pair of balances in his hand.

The witnesses note the third seal — a black horse and a pair of balances, betokening scarcity and famine; food doled out by careful weight, the black colour fitting the gloom of want.

So the witnesses make the verse the black horse of scarcity and famine. The lesson the expositors draw is that the third seal, with its black horse and balances, betokens famine, food weighed out in scant measure. The witnesses note the third seal: "when he had opened the third seal" — the third creature summons; the third seal; the the creature; the summons; the come and see; the calls. The expositors observe the black horse: "lo a black horse" — black, the colour of mourning; the black horse; the black; the mourning; the distress; the gloom. The witnesses press the balances: "a pair of balances in his hand" — scales betokening scarcity; the balances; the scales; the scarcity; the dearth; the the rider. The expositors draw the lesson on the weighed food: in famine food is doled out by weight; the weighed food; the doled; the by weight; the rationed; the famine. The witnesses observe the the gloom of want: the black colour fits the grief of want; the the gloom of want; the the black; the grief; the distress; the famine. The expositors note the famine after war: following war, the famine war breeds; the famine after war; the after war; the breeds; the scant measure; the want. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when he had opened the third seal lo a black horse, the he that sat on him had a pair of balances in his hand, and the black horse of scarcity. The verse thus records the black horse of scarcity and famine — teaching that the third seal betokens famine, food weighed out in scant measure.
6:
"And I heard a voice in the midst of the four beasts say, A measure of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny; and see thou hurt not the oil and the wine." The witnesses note the voice declaring the prices of famine and the sparing of oil and wine. Gill notes the voice in the midst of the four beasts is rather that of Christ, who was in the midst of them; the words declare the dearth and scarcity of provisions. The witnesses note John hearing a voice declare a measure of wheat for a penny and three of barley for a penny, yet bidding hurt not the oil and the wine; the high famine-prices of grain, with a measure of mercy in the sparing of oil and wine — that the vision of the third seal is completed by a voice (which Gill takes to be Christ's, "in the midst of the four beasts") proclaiming the famine-prices: "a measure of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny" (a day's wage spent for a single day's bread, the very mark of scarcity and dearth) — yet with a limit set: "see thou hurt not the oil and the wine"; the witnesses note the touch of mercy in the judgment — though grain is scarce and dear, the "oil and the wine" are spared, so that the famine, severe as it is, is not utter and total destruction; God's judgments, even in the dearth, are tempered and bounded; see thou hurt not the oil and the wine. The witnesses note a measure of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny. The witnesses note see thou hurt not the oil and the wine.

The witnesses note the famine-prices declared — a day's wage for a day's bread — yet the oil and wine spared; the judgment, severe as it is, tempered and bounded, not utter destruction.

So the witnesses make the verse the famine-prices with the sparing of oil and wine. The lesson the expositors draw is that the third seal brings dear scarcity, yet the judgment is tempered, the oil and wine spared. The witnesses note the voice: "a voice in the midst of the four beasts" — Gill notes Christ's voice in the midst; the voice; the Christ's; the in the midst; the the beasts; the declares. The expositors observe the famine-prices: "a measure of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny" — a day's wage for a day's bread; the famine-prices; the a day's wage; the a day's bread; the scarcity; the dearth. The witnesses press the mark of dearth: the very mark of scarcity and dearth; the mark of dearth; the scarcity; the high prices; the want; the famine. The expositors draw the lesson on the hurt not: "see thou hurt not the oil and the wine" — the oil and wine spared; the hurt not; the the oil; the the wine; the spared; the limit. The witnesses observe the touch of mercy: a touch of mercy in the judgment; the touch of mercy; the the judgment; the spared; the tempered; the bounded. The expositors note the not utter destruction: the famine is severe but not total; the not utter destruction; the severe; the not total; the tempered; the bounded. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the a measure of wheat for a penny and three measures of barley for a penny, the see thou hurt not the oil and the wine, and the famine-prices. The verse thus records the famine-prices with the sparing of oil and wine — teaching that the third seal brings scarcity, yet the judgment is tempered, the oil and wine spared.
7:
"And when he had opened the fourth seal, I heard the voice of the fourth beast say, Come and see." The witnesses note the opening of the fourth seal and the fourth creature's summons. Barnes notes that, as in the previous cases, the fourth living creature — the flying eagle — calls attention to the new vision now to be disclosed. The witnesses note John hearing the voice of the fourth living creature say "Come and see" as the Lamb opened the fourth seal; the fourth creature summoning the seer to the next scene of judgment — that John, at the opening of "the fourth seal," hears "the voice of the fourth beast" (the fourth of the living creatures, the flying eagle) "say, Come and see" — the same orderly summons as before, now from the last of the four creatures; the witnesses note the continuing pattern — each of the four living creatures in turn has called the seer to behold a seal opened, and this fourth and final summons introduces the most terrible of the four horsemen, the pale horse of death that now follows; so that the regular, solemn unfolding goes on, the fourth creature opening the way to the climax of the four riders; and when he had opened the fourth seal, I heard the voice of the fourth beast say, Come and see. The witnesses note when he had opened the fourth seal. The witnesses note I heard the voice of the fourth beast say, Come and see.

The witnesses note the fourth seal opened and the fourth creature's summons — the last of the four living creatures introducing the most terrible horseman, the pale horse of death.

So the witnesses make the verse the fourth creature summoning to the next scene. The lesson the expositors draw is that the fourth and last creature calls the seer to behold, introducing the climax of the four riders. The witnesses note the fourth seal: "when he had opened the fourth seal" — the Lamb opens the fourth; the fourth seal; the the Lamb; the opens; the in turn; the unfolds. The expositors observe the fourth beast: "the voice of the fourth beast" — Barnes notes the flying eagle; the fourth beast; the the flying eagle; the the creature; the the last; the summons. The witnesses press the come and see: "say, Come and see" — the orderly summons; the come and see; the the summons; the behold; the calls; the attend. The expositors draw the lesson on the each in turn: each of the four creatures in turn has called; the each in turn; the the four; the called; the the seer; the behold. The witnesses observe the most terrible: this introduces the most terrible horseman; the most terrible; the the pale horse; the death; the the climax; the follows. The expositors note the climax of the riders: the way is opened to the climax of the four riders; the climax of the riders; the the four riders; the the climax; the death; the follows. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when he had opened the fourth seal, the I heard the voice of the fourth beast say Come and see, and the summons to death. The verse thus records the fourth creature summoning to the next scene — teaching that the last creature calls the seer to behold, introducing the climax of the four riders.
8:
"And I looked, and behold a pale horse: and his name that sat on him was Death, and Hell followed with him. And power was given unto them over the fourth part of the earth, to kill with sword, and with hunger, and with death, and with the beasts of the earth." The witnesses note the fourth seal — the pale horse, Death and Hell, with power over a fourth of the earth. Gill notes the pale horse may be an emblem of the state of the church, pale with hypocrisy and superstition, paving the way for the man of sin. The witnesses note John seeing a pale horse, its rider named Death with Hell following, given power over a fourth of the earth to kill by sword, hunger, death, and beasts; the pale horse of death gathering the previous plagues into one fearful judgment — that the fourth seal discloses the most dreadful vision: "a pale horse" "and his name that sat on him was Death, and Hell followed with him" (Death the rider, the grave following the slain) — "and power was given unto them over the fourth part of the earth, to kill with sword, and with hunger, and with death, and with the beasts of the earth"; the witnesses note that this rider gathers up all the previous plagues — sword, hunger, pestilence, and wild beasts — into one comprehensive judgment over a "fourth part of the earth"; yet the destruction is limited to a fourth, bounded by God; his name that sat on him was Death, and Hell followed with him. The witnesses note behold a pale horse: and his name that sat on him was Death, and Hell followed with him. The witnesses note power was given unto them over the fourth part of the earth, to kill with sword, and with hunger, and with death, and with the beasts of the earth.

The witnesses note the pale horse of death — its rider Death, with Hell following — given power over a fourth of the earth to kill by sword, hunger, pestilence, and beasts; yet the destruction bounded.

So the witnesses make the verse the pale horse gathering all the plagues. The lesson the expositors draw is that the fourth seal brings death by every plague over a fourth of the earth, yet the judgment is bounded. The witnesses note the pale horse: "behold a pale horse" — Gill notes pale, the colour of a corpse; the pale horse; the pale; the a corpse; the sickness; the death. The expositors observe the Death and Hell: "his name that sat on him was Death, and Hell followed with him" — Death the rider, the grave following; the Death and Hell; the the rider; the the grave; the the slain. The witnesses press the fourth part: "power... over the fourth part of the earth" — over a fourth of the earth; the fourth part; the a fourth; the limited. The expositors draw the lesson on the the four plagues: "to kill with sword, and with hunger, and with death, and with the beasts" — sword, famine, pestilence, beasts; the the four plagues; the sword; the the beasts. The witnesses observe the gathers the plagues: this rider gathers all the previous plagues into one; the gathers the plagues; the one judgment; the comprehensive. The expositors note the bounded: yet limited to a fourth, bounded by God; the bounded; the a fourth; the limited; the the judgment. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the his name that sat on him was Death and Hell followed with him, and the pale horse of death. The verse thus records the pale horse gathering all the plagues — teaching that the fourth seal brings death by every plague over a fourth of the earth, yet bounded.
9:
"And when he had opened the fifth seal, I saw under the altar the souls of them that were slain for the word of God, and for the testimony which they held:" The witnesses note the fifth seal — the souls of the martyrs under the altar. Gill notes this was a dark time both for the church and the ministry; the fifth seal shows the souls of the martyrs under the altar, slain for the word of God and the testimony they held. The witnesses note John seeing, at the fifth seal, under the altar the souls of those slain for the word of God and their testimony; the martyrs' souls beneath the altar, conscious and alive after death — that the fifth seal, unlike the first four, shows no horse and rider but a scene in heaven: "I saw under the altar the souls of them that were slain for the word of God, and for the testimony which they held"; the witnesses note two things — first, the place and state of the martyrs: their "souls" are "under the altar" (a place of honour and safety near God, the altar speaking of their sacrifice), and they are seen as living, conscious souls after death, proving the soul does not perish or sleep with the body — and second, the cause of their death: they were "slain for the word of God, and for the testimony which they held," martyred not for crime but for the faith they confessed; so that the fifth seal honours the holy martyrs, whose souls live with God; I saw under the altar the souls of them that were slain for the word of God. The witnesses note I saw under the altar the souls of them that were slain for the word of God. The witnesses note and for the testimony which they held.

The witnesses note the fifth seal — the souls of the martyrs under the altar, slain for the word of God; living, conscious souls after death, honoured near God for the faith they confessed.

So the witnesses make the verse the martyrs' souls beneath the altar. The lesson the expositors draw is that the martyrs' souls live, conscious, under the altar near God, slain for the word and their testimony. The witnesses note the fifth seal: "when he had opened the fifth seal" — Gill notes a dark time, no horse here; the fifth seal; the no horse; the a scene in heaven; the the martyrs; the dark time. The expositors observe the under the altar: "I saw under the altar the souls" — a place of honour near God; the under the altar; the honour; the near God; the safety; the the altar. The witnesses press the souls living: the souls are living, conscious after death; the souls living; the conscious; the after death; the not perish; the not sleep. The expositors draw the lesson on the slain for the word: "slain for the word of God" — martyred for the faith; the slain for the word; the martyred; the the faith; the for the word; the for Christ. The witnesses observe the testimony held: "and for the testimony which they held" — for the faith they confessed; the testimony held; the the faith; the confessed; the held; the martyred. The expositors note the honours the martyrs: the fifth seal honours the holy martyrs; the honours the martyrs; the the holy martyrs; the live with God; the honoured; the the souls. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I saw under the altar the souls of them that were slain for the word of God, the and for the testimony which they held, and the martyrs' souls. The verse thus records the martyrs' souls beneath the altar — teaching that the martyrs' souls live, conscious, near God, slain for the word and their testimony.
10:
"And they cried with a loud voice, saying, How long, O Lord, holy and true, dost thou not judge and avenge our blood on them that dwell on the earth?" The witnesses note the martyrs' cry for the avenging of their blood. Gill notes they cried with great ardour and fervency, being very pressing and importunate; which shows they were awake and not asleep, that the soul does not sleep with the body in the grave. The witnesses note John hearing the souls cry with a loud voice, "How long, O Lord, holy and true, dost thou not judge and avenge our blood?"; the martyrs' importunate cry for God's righteous judgment on their persecutors — that the souls under the altar are heard to cry: "with a loud voice" (earnest and importunate, proof again that they live and are conscious) "How long, O Lord, holy and true, dost thou not judge and avenge our blood on them that dwell on the earth?"; the witnesses note the spirit of the cry — it is no private vindictiveness, but an appeal to God as "holy and true" (whose holiness must punish sin and whose truth must keep his word) for the righteous avenging of the innocent blood shed by their persecutors; the "how long" voices the longing of the suffering church for the day of God's vindication, when he will judge the world and avenge his saints; how long, O Lord, holy and true, dost thou not judge and avenge our blood? The witnesses note how long, O Lord, holy and true. The witnesses note dost thou not judge and avenge our blood on them that dwell on the earth?

The witnesses note the martyrs' cry "How long?" — an appeal to God as holy and true for the righteous avenging of innocent blood; the longing of the suffering church for God's vindication.

So the witnesses make the verse the martyrs' cry for righteous judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is that the martyrs' souls cry to the holy and true God for the avenging of their innocent blood. The witnesses note the loud voice: "they cried with a loud voice" — Gill notes earnest and importunate, proof they live; the loud voice; the earnest; the importunate; the live; the conscious. The expositors observe the how long: "How long, O Lord" — the longing cry; the how long; the the longing; the the cry; the for vindication; the the day. The witnesses press the holy and true: "holy and true" — God whose holiness punishes sin, whose truth keeps his word; the holy and true; the holiness; the punishes; the truth; the keeps his word. The expositors draw the lesson on the judge and avenge: "dost thou not judge and avenge our blood" — the righteous avenging of innocent blood; the judge and avenge; the righteous; the innocent blood; the the persecutors; the vindication. The witnesses observe the no vindictiveness: no private vindictiveness but an appeal for justice; the no vindictiveness; the not private; the an appeal; the for justice; the righteous. The expositors note the the suffering church: the longing of the suffering church for vindication; the the suffering church; the longing; the vindication; the the day; the God's. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the how long O Lord holy and true, the dost thou not judge and avenge our blood on them that dwell on the earth, and the martyrs' cry. The verse thus records the martyrs' cry for righteous judgment — teaching that the martyrs' souls cry to the holy and true God for the avenging of their innocent blood.
11:
"And white robes were given unto every one of them; and it was said unto them, that they should rest yet for a little season, until their fellowservants also and their brethren, that should be killed as they were, should be fulfilled." The witnesses note the white robes given to the martyrs and the bidding to rest a little longer. Gill notes white robes were given to each as a token of their innocence — that they did not suffer for any capital crime or immorality, but were righteous in God's sight. The witnesses note John seeing white robes given to each martyr, and they were bidden rest yet a little season until the full number of their fellow-martyrs should be completed; the answer to the martyrs' cry — robes of honour now, vindication when the number is full — that John records God's gracious answer to the martyrs' "how long": first, "white robes were given unto every one of them" (a token of their innocence, acceptance, and present blessedness, the white speaking of their purity and victory) — and second, a word of patience: "it was said unto them, that they should rest yet for a little season, until their fellowservants also and their brethren, that should be killed as they were, should be fulfilled"; the witnesses note the gracious delay — the full avenging is not yet, but only "a little season," for God has a fixed number of martyrs still to be slain before the day of his vindication; so the martyrs are comforted with present honour and rest, and assured the day of reckoning will surely come when their number is complete; they should rest yet for a little season. The witnesses note white robes were given unto every one of them. The witnesses note they should rest yet for a little season, until their fellowservants also and their brethren... should be fulfilled.

The witnesses note the answer to the martyrs' cry — white robes of honour now, and the bidding to rest a little longer until the full number of martyrs is complete; comfort with present rest and sure vindication.

So the witnesses make the verse the answer of robes and rest to the martyrs. The lesson the expositors draw is that the martyrs are given robes of honour and bidden rest until the number of their fellows is complete. The witnesses note the white robes: "white robes were given unto every one of them" — Gill notes a token of their innocence; the white robes; the innocence; the acceptance; the purity; the victory. The expositors observe the present blessedness: a token of their present blessedness; the present blessedness; the honour; the acceptance; the white; the victory. The witnesses press the rest a little: "they should rest yet for a little season" — bidden to rest a little longer; the rest a little; the a little season; the rest; the wait; the patience. The expositors draw the lesson on the until fulfilled: "until their fellowservants... should be fulfilled" — until the number is complete; the until fulfilled; the the number; the complete; the the fellow-martyrs; the still to be slain. The witnesses observe the fixed number: God has a fixed number of martyrs yet to be slain; the fixed number; the of martyrs; the yet to be slain; the the day; the appointed. The expositors note the sure vindication: comforted with rest, assured the day will come; the sure vindication; the the day; the reckoning. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the they should rest yet for a little season until their fellowservants also and their brethren should be fulfilled, and the answer of robes and rest. The verse thus records the answer of robes and rest to the martyrs — teaching that the martyrs are given honour and bidden rest until the number of their fellows is complete.
12:
"And I beheld when he had opened the sixth seal, and, lo, there was a great earthquake; and the sun became black as sackcloth of hair, and the moon became as blood;" The witnesses note the sixth seal — the great earthquake and the darkening of sun and moon. Gill notes interpreters much differ about this seal; some refer it to the destruction of Jerusalem, but it is more largely applied to the great shaking and overthrow of the heathen Roman empire under Constantine. The witnesses note John seeing, at the sixth seal, a great earthquake, the sun blackened, and the moon turned to blood; the convulsion of the heavens and earth at the sixth seal — a vision of overturning judgment — that the sixth seal opens a scene of cosmic upheaval: "a great earthquake" (a violent shaking, the very frame of things convulsed) "and the sun became black as sackcloth of hair, and the moon became as blood"; the witnesses note the interpretive variety the commentators set out — some refer this convulsion to the destruction of Jerusalem, others to the day of judgment, but many (Gill, Barnes) to the great overthrow of the pagan Roman empire, the "earthquake" and the darkening of the great lights of heaven figuring the fall of rulers and the collapse of the old order; whichever the application, the imagery is of God's awful judgment shaking the established powers to their foundations; the sun became black as sackcloth of hair, and the moon became as blood. The witnesses note when he had opened the sixth seal... there was a great earthquake. The witnesses note the sun became black as sackcloth of hair, and the moon became as blood.

The witnesses note the sixth seal's cosmic upheaval — a great earthquake, the sun blackened, the moon as blood; imagery of God's awful judgment shaking the established powers to their foundations.

So the witnesses make the verse the convulsion of heaven and earth. The lesson the expositors draw is that the sixth seal opens a scene of overturning judgment, the powers shaken to their foundations. The witnesses note the great earthquake: "there was a great earthquake" — Gill notes a violent shaking; the great earthquake; the violent; the shaking; the convulsed; the the frame. The expositors observe the sun black: "the sun became black as sackcloth of hair" — the sun darkened; the sun black; the darkened; the as sackcloth; the the great light; the obscured. The witnesses press the moon as blood: "the moon became as blood" — the moon turned to blood; the moon as blood; the the moon; the to blood; the the light; the obscured. The expositors present the readings: the witnesses note the variety — some refer it to Jerusalem's fall, some to the day of judgment, many to the overthrow of pagan Rome. The witnesses observe the fall of powers: the imagery figures the fall of rulers and the old order; the fall of powers; the the rulers; the the old order; the collapse; the overthrow. The expositors note the awful judgment: God's awful judgment shaking the powers; the awful judgment; the shaking; the the powers; the the foundations; the overturning. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when he had opened the sixth seal there was a great earthquake, the the sun became black as sackcloth of hair and the moon became as blood, and the cosmic upheaval. The verse thus records the convulsion of heaven and earth — teaching that the sixth seal opens a scene of overturning judgment, the powers shaken to their foundations.
13:
"And the stars of heaven fell unto the earth, even as a fig tree casteth her untimely figs, when she is shaken of a mighty wind." The witnesses note the falling of the stars of heaven like figs in a wind. Gill notes (in the historicist application) that all the other inferior deities lost their esteem and honour, the idol temples being opened and the idols exposed; the stars representing those that were cast down from their dignity. The witnesses note John seeing the stars of heaven fall to earth like untimely figs shaken from a tree by a mighty wind; the falling stars figuring the casting down of the high and exalted — that the sixth seal's upheaval continues: "the stars of heaven fell unto the earth, even as a fig tree casteth her untimely figs, when she is shaken of a mighty wind"; the witnesses note the vivid figure — as a fig tree, violently shaken by a great wind, drops its unripe figs in a shower to the ground, so the "stars of heaven" fall; in the prophetic language, "stars" stand for the high, the rulers, princes, and dignitaries; their falling figures the casting down from their place of all that was exalted and bright in the old order — the great ones brought low in the day of God's judgment; so that nothing high stands when God shakes the heavens; the stars of heaven fell unto the earth. The witnesses note the stars of heaven fell unto the earth. The witnesses note as a fig tree casteth her untimely figs, when she is shaken of a mighty wind.

The witnesses note the stars of heaven falling like untimely figs in a wind — figuring the casting down of the high and exalted; nothing high standing when God shakes the heavens.

So the witnesses make the verse the falling stars figuring the high cast down. The lesson the expositors draw is that the stars, the high and exalted, fall in God's judgment, nothing lofty standing when he shakes the heavens. The witnesses note the stars fell: "the stars of heaven fell unto the earth" — Gill notes the high cast down; the stars fell; the the high; the cast down; the exalted; the brought low. The expositors observe the the figure: "as a fig tree casteth her untimely figs" — the vivid figure; the the figure; the the fig tree; the the figs; the dropped; the shaken. The witnesses press the mighty wind: "when she is shaken of a mighty wind" — violently shaken by a great wind; the mighty wind; the violently; the shaken; the the wind; the dropped. The expositors draw the lesson on the stars are rulers: "stars" stand for rulers and dignitaries; the stars are rulers; the rulers; the princes; the dignitaries; the the high. The witnesses observe the cast down: the high cast down from their place; the cast down; the the high; the their place; the brought low; the the exalted. The expositors note the nothing high stands: nothing high stands when God shakes; the nothing high stands; the the heavens; the shaken; the God; the judgment. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the stars of heaven fell unto the earth, the as a fig tree casteth her untimely figs when she is shaken of a mighty wind, and the falling stars. The verse thus records the falling stars figuring the high cast down — teaching that the high and exalted fall in God's judgment.
14:
"And the heaven departed as a scroll when it is rolled together; and every mountain and island were moved out of their places." The witnesses note the heaven rolling up like a scroll and the moving of mountains and islands. Gill notes the manner of writing formerly was on rolls of parchment, hence a "volume"; in like manner the Roman empire (in the historicist reading) was rolled up and its heaven departed. The witnesses note John seeing the heaven depart as a scroll rolled together, and every mountain and island moved out of its place; the total upheaval — the very heavens removed and the firmest things displaced — that the sixth seal reaches its height: "the heaven departed as a scroll when it is rolled together" (the visible heaven rolled up and removed, as a parchment scroll is rolled shut and put away) "and every mountain and island were moved out of their places"; the witnesses note the completeness of the overturning — not only are sun, moon, and stars darkened and fallen, but the very "heaven" is rolled away, and "every mountain and island" — the most fixed, stable, immovable things — is shaken "out of their places"; in the figure, the whole established order, from the highest to the most settled, is utterly removed at God's judgment; so that the imagery declares that nothing, however fixed, can stand before the day of his wrath; the heaven departed as a scroll when it is rolled together. The witnesses note the heaven departed as a scroll when it is rolled together. The witnesses note every mountain and island were moved out of their places.

The witnesses note the heaven rolling up like a scroll and every mountain and island moved — the total overturning, the most fixed things displaced; nothing standing before the day of God's wrath.

So the witnesses make the verse the total upheaval of the established order. The lesson the expositors draw is that the very heavens are rolled away and the firmest things displaced, nothing standing before God's judgment. The witnesses note the heaven departed: "the heaven departed as a scroll" — Gill notes rolled up like a parchment; the heaven departed; the rolled up; the a parchment; the put away; the removed. The expositors observe the rolled together: "when it is rolled together" — rolled shut and removed; the rolled together; the rolled shut; the removed; the the scroll; the put away. The witnesses press the mountains moved: "every mountain and island were moved" — the most fixed things shaken; the mountains moved; the every mountain; the every island; the fixed; the shaken. The expositors draw the lesson on the out of their places: "out of their places" — displaced from their settled places; the out of their places; the displaced; the the settled; the moved; the removed. The witnesses observe the completeness: the completeness of the overturning, highest to most settled; the completeness; the highest; the most settled; the removed; the all. The expositors note the nothing stands: nothing, however fixed, can stand before God's wrath; the nothing stands; the however fixed; the God's wrath; the the day; the removed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the heaven departed as a scroll when it is rolled together, the every mountain and island were moved out of their places, and the total upheaval. The verse thus records the total upheaval of the established order — teaching that the very heavens are rolled away and the firmest things displaced before God's judgment.
15:
"And the kings of the earth, and the great men, and the rich men, and the chief captains, and the mighty men, and every bondman, and every free man, hid themselves in the dens and in the rocks of the mountains;" The witnesses note the universal terror that drives all ranks to hide. Barnes notes the design of this and the following verses is to depict the universal consternation — the dismay that would seize upon all classes of men. Gill notes the kings of the earth are the Roman emperors and chief magistrates. The witnesses note John seeing the kings, great men, rich, captains, mighty, and every bondman and free man hide in the dens and rocks of the mountains; the universal terror in which men of every rank seek to hide from God's wrath — that the sixth seal's judgment falls on all sorts of men, and John names them in a sweeping list, from the highest to the lowest: "the kings of the earth, and the great men, and the rich men, and the chief captains, and the mighty men, and every bondman, and every free man"; the witnesses note the universality and the terror — no rank or class is exempt, neither king nor slave; and all alike, struck with dread, "hid themselves in the dens and in the rocks of the mountains," seeking any shelter from the coming wrath; so that the day of God's judgment levels all distinctions, and the mighty and the lowly are filled with one common terror, fleeing to hide; hid themselves in the dens and in the rocks of the mountains. The witnesses note the kings of the earth, and the great men, and the rich men, and the chief captains. The witnesses note hid themselves in the dens and in the rocks of the mountains.

The witnesses note the universal terror — kings, great men, captains, bondmen, and free all hiding in the dens and rocks; the day of judgment levelling all distinctions in one common dread.

So the witnesses make the verse the universal terror driving all ranks to hide. The lesson the expositors draw is that men of every rank, struck with dread, seek to hide from God's coming wrath. The witnesses note the kings and great men: "the kings of the earth, and the great men, and the rich men" — Gill notes the highest ranks; the kings and great men; the the kings; the the great; the the rich; the the highest. The expositors observe the every rank: "the chief captains... every bondman, and every free man" — from highest to lowest; the every rank; the captains; the the mighty; the bondman; the free man. The witnesses press the no exemption: no rank or class is exempt; the no exemption; the neither king nor slave; the all; the every class; the alike. The expositors draw the lesson on the hid themselves: "hid themselves in the dens and in the rocks" — seeking any shelter; the hid themselves; the the dens; the the rocks; the shelter; the hide. The witnesses observe the struck with dread: all alike struck with dread; the struck with dread; the terror; the all; the the wrath; the flee. The expositors note the levels all: the day of judgment levels all distinctions; the levels all; the all distinctions; the the mighty; the the lowly; the one terror. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the kings of the earth and the great men and the rich men and the chief captains, the hid themselves in the dens and in the rocks of the mountains, and the universal terror. The verse thus records the universal terror driving all ranks to hide — teaching that men of every rank seek to hide from God's coming wrath.
16:
"And said to the mountains and rocks, Fall on us, and hide us from the face of him that sitteth on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb:" The witnesses note the despairing cry to the mountains to fall and hide them from God and the Lamb. Gill notes they chose death rather than life, calling on the mountains and rocks to fall on them, so great was their terror and despair. The witnesses note John hearing them cry to the mountains and rocks, "Fall on us, and hide us from the face of him that sitteth on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb"; the despair that prefers death to facing the wrath of God and the Lamb — that the terror reaches such a height that men cry to the very mountains and rocks to crush them: "Fall on us, and hide us"; the witnesses note the depth of their despair — they would rather be buried alive under falling rocks than face "him that sitteth on the throne" (God the Judge) and "the wrath of the Lamb"; the witnesses note the striking phrase "the wrath of the Lamb" — the very One who is meek and gentle, the Lamb slain, is now the object of terror, for his wrath, when at last it comes, is the more dreadful for the patience and gentleness so long despised; so that the wicked, who would not bow to the Lamb's grace, now flee in horror from the Lamb's wrath; hide us... from the wrath of the Lamb. The witnesses note Fall on us, and hide us from the face of him that sitteth on the throne. The witnesses note and from the wrath of the Lamb.

The witnesses note the despairing cry to the mountains — "Fall on us, and hide us" from God and "the wrath of the Lamb"; the meek Lamb's wrath the more dreadful for the grace so long despised.

So the witnesses make the verse the despairing cry to be hidden from the Lamb's wrath. The lesson the expositors draw is that men prefer death under falling rocks to facing God the Judge and the wrath of the Lamb. The witnesses note the fall on us: "Fall on us, and hide us" — Gill notes they chose death over life; the fall on us; the chose death; the over life; the the rocks; the crush. The expositors observe the from his face: "from the face of him that sitteth on the throne" — from God the Judge; the from his face; the God; the the Judge; the the throne; the flee. The witnesses press the wrath of the Lamb: "and from the wrath of the Lamb" — the wrath of the Lamb; the wrath of the Lamb; the the Lamb; the wrath; the terror; the dreadful. The expositors draw the lesson on the meek Lamb: the meek Lamb is now the object of terror; the meek Lamb; the meek; the the object; the terror; the slain. The witnesses observe the grace despised: his wrath more dreadful for the grace so long despised; the grace despised; the the patience; the gentleness; the despised; the dreadful. The expositors note the flee in horror: the wicked flee in horror from the Lamb's wrath; the flee in horror; the the wicked; the the Lamb's wrath; the would not bow; the now flee. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Fall on us and hide us from the face of him that sitteth on the throne, the and from the wrath of the Lamb, and the despairing cry. The verse thus records the despairing cry to be hidden from the Lamb's wrath — teaching that men prefer death to facing God the Judge and the wrath of the Lamb.
17:
"For the great day of his wrath is come; and who shall be able to stand?" The witnesses note the declaration that the great day of wrath is come, and the question who can stand. Gill notes the great day of his wrath is come, both of him that sits on the throne and of the Lamb, against the heathen gods and persecutors, in answer to the cry of the martyrs. The witnesses note John recording the cry, "For the great day of his wrath is come; and who shall be able to stand?"; the terrible day of wrath arrived, before which none can stand — that the despairing flight of the wicked is closed with the reason and the unanswerable question: "for the great day of his wrath is come" (the long-delayed day of God's and the Lamb's righteous anger has at last arrived, in answer to the martyrs' "how long") "and who shall be able to stand?"; the witnesses note the force of the question — it is a cry of utter hopelessness, owning that none can endure or survive that day, none can stand before the wrath of the almighty God and the Lamb; the very persecutors who once seemed all-powerful now confess they cannot stand; so that the sixth seal ends with the overthrow of the proud and the demonstration that no power can resist the day of God's wrath, a solemn answer to the martyrs' prayer and a warning to all; who shall be able to stand? The witnesses note for the great day of his wrath is come. The witnesses note and who shall be able to stand?

The witnesses note the great day of wrath arrived and the unanswerable question "who shall be able to stand?" — a cry of utter hopelessness, none able to endure the wrath of God and the Lamb.

So the witnesses make the verse the day of wrath before which none can stand. The lesson the expositors draw is that the great day of God's wrath has come, and none can stand before it. The witnesses note the great day: "the great day of his wrath is come" — Gill notes the day of God's and the Lamb's anger arrived; the great day; the his wrath; the come; the the Lamb; the arrived. The expositors observe the answer to the martyrs: in answer to the martyrs' "how long"; the answer to the martyrs; the how long; the the cry; the avenged; the the day. The witnesses press the who can stand: "and who shall be able to stand?" — the unanswerable question; the who can stand; the unanswerable; the the question; the none; the hopelessness. The expositors draw the lesson on the none endure: none can endure or survive that day; the none endure; the survive; the that day; the the wrath; the cannot. The witnesses observe the persecutors confess: the persecutors confess they cannot stand; the persecutors confess; the once powerful; the cannot stand; the the wrath; the overthrown. The expositors note the no power resists: no power can resist the day of God's wrath; the no power resists; the resist; the God's wrath; the the day; the overthrown. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for the great day of his wrath is come, the and who shall be able to stand, and the day of wrath. The verse thus records the day of wrath before which none can stand — teaching that the great day of God's wrath has come, and none can stand before it.
